title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: This PR has several serious issues that block merge. A debug `console.log` serializes the full charge request body — including card number and CVC — to stdout, violating PCI-DSS. A logic bug means `discount_amount` is always 0 in the response. Coupon errors are silently swallowed with a `// TODO` catch. No unit tests exist for the new pricing logic, which is flagged as a known TODO in the PR description. Beyond the blockers: `couponCode` bypasses Zod validation entirely, the response mixes snake_case into a camelCase API, `as any` disables type safety on a payment response object, and the entire couponDiscount module is a Strategy+Factory+abstract-class hierarchy for what is fundamentally a six-row lookup table. Several naming and style issues (`doStuff`, `var`, `==`, `tmp`, `result2`) compound the readability problems.

findings.title: Full charge body (PAN + CVC) logged to stdout
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData))` serializes the entire request body — which includes `card.number` and `card.cvc` — to stdout on every charge attempt. PCI-DSS forbids plaintext PAN in logs and absolutely prohibits storing or logging CVC anywhere, ever. The project ships a pino logger with a redaction allowlist precisely to prevent this; this one line defeats that control completely. Delete this line. If you need request tracing, log only non-sensitive identifiers (e.g. `customerId`, `currency`) through the injected `logger`.

findings.title: `discount_amount` is always 0 — original amount overwritten before it is used
findings.message: Line 23 sets `payload.amount = finalAmount` (the *discounted* amount). Line 35 then computes `response.discount_amount = payload.amount - finalAmount`, which is `finalAmount - finalAmount = 0` every time. The original amount is lost before the subtraction. Fix: capture the original amount before the try block — `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` — and use `originalAmount - finalAmount` in the response. Also reconsider whether `payload.amount` needs to be mutated at all; passing `finalAmount` explicitly to the charge call (which line 29 already does) is sufficient.

findings.title: Coupon errors silently swallowed — `catch (e) { // TODO }`
findings.message: Any exception thrown by `doStuff` (e.g. unexpected input, future runtime error) is caught here and discarded. The charge then proceeds with the *original* amount, and the caller gets a 201 with no indication that coupon application failed. A swallowed error means a bad coupon, an undefined input, or a real bug all look like success. Either handle it meaningfully (log the error and return a 422 with `coupon_invalid`) or rethrow and let the outer error handler deal with it — but never silently continue.

findings.title: No unit tests for new discount/pricing logic
findings.message: The PR description explicitly marks 'Add unit tests for the coupon math' as unchecked and deferred to a follow-up. For money-adjacent branching logic this is a blocker, not a backlog item. We need tests for: each coupon code applies the correct discount, unknown codes return the original amount, `couponCode: undefined` is a no-op, and the edge case where a fixed discount exceeds the charge amount (currently `FixedCouponStrategy.apply` can return a negative number — e.g. $10 off a $3 charge). These are exactly the cases that silently go wrong in prod without test coverage.

findings.title: `couponCode` extracted from raw body before Zod validation — no length or pattern check
findings.message: `couponCode` is read directly off `req.body` before `chargeSchema.parse()` runs, which means Zod never sees it. Even if `chargeSchema` were updated to include `couponCode`, the value used downstream is the raw, unvalidated string. An attacker can send an arbitrarily long or specially crafted string. At minimum, add `couponCode: z.string().min(1).max(32).regex(/^[A-Z0-9]+$/).optional()` to the schema and read it from `payload`, not from `theData`.

findings.title: `const response: any = transaction` disables type safety on a payment response
findings.message: `as any` (via the `any` annotation) silences the compiler on the object that gets serialized to the client. If `transaction` ever gains a `password_hash`, `card_number`, or any other sensitive field, there is nothing left to catch it. Model the response type instead: `interface ChargeResponse { id: string; amount: number; discountAmount: number; couponCode?: string; /* ... */ }` and construct it explicitly. Let the compiler tell you what you're exposing.

findings.title: Response fields `discount_amount` / `coupon_code` use snake_case in a camelCase API
findings.message: The rest of this API uses camelCase (e.g. `customerId`, `createdAt`). Adding `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` forces every consumer to special-case this endpoint, and it's the kind of casing mismatch that silently produces `undefined` the first time someone writes `res.discountAmount`. Use `discountAmount` and `couponCode` to match the house convention.

findings.title: Strategy + Factory + abstract class for a six-row static lookup is severe over-engineering
`AbstractCouponStrategy` is an interface wearing an abstract-class costume
findings.message: `AbstractCouponStrategy` has exactly one abstract method and zero concrete behavior. That is the definition of an interface. An abstract class earns its keep by providing shared state or concrete methods — this one provides neither, and it forces single inheritance on every implementor for no reason. If the strategy pattern stays, change this to `interface CouponStrategy { apply(amount: number): number; }` and drop `extends AbstractCouponStrategy` from both subclasses.

findings.title: Exported function named `doStuff` — meaningless name on a public API
findings.message: `doStuff` is the exported entry point used by the controller. It tells the caller nothing about what it does, what it returns, or what the side effects are. Rename it to something honest: `applyCoupon(amount, couponCode)` describes both arguments and the operation. Same issue with `theData` in the controller (a pointless rename of `req.body`), and `tmp` / `result2` / `x` inside this file — but an *exported* name is the one that most needs to be correct.

findings.title: `var` and `==` used throughout; rest of the codebase uses `const`/`let` and `===`
findings.message: `var x` (line 16) and `var tmp` (line 58) should be `const`. `code == 'WELCOME10'` (line 34 and throughout the factory) and `tmp == null` (line 59) should use `===`. These aren't style preferences — `var` has function scope and hoisting semantics that differ from `let`/`const`, and `==` coerces types (`null == undefined` is `true`). Both are detectable by the linter (`prefer-const`, `eqeqeq`). Fix them before more code builds on this file.

findings.title: `console.log` for unknown coupon code — should use structured logger
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode)` bypasses the pino structured logger and its redaction rules. Beyond bypassing log levels and structured fields, this also logs raw user-supplied coupon codes to stdout without any sanitization. Use the injected `logger.warn({ couponCode }, 'unknown coupon code')` instead (assuming logger is threaded through, or passed as a parameter).

findings.title: Commented-out `FREESHIP` block — dead code, delete it
findings.message: Nobody knows if this is a plan, a rollback, or a fossil. `FixedCouponStrategy(0)` for free shipping doesn't even make sense in the current model (it would reduce the charge amount by $0). Git history is the backup — delete it.

findings.title: `const theData = req.body` is a pointless rename
findings.message: `req.body` is a perfectly clear name. Aliasing it to `theData` just adds a line and makes readers wonder if there's a difference. Delete `theData` and pass `req.body` directly to `chargeSchema.parse()`.
